oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Managing controlled substances in ambulatory practice
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Capel, Michael
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: controlled substance
ambulatory practice
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Controlled drugs are a useful tool for chemical restraint when working with livestock; however, proper management can be a significant challenge for large animal ambulatory practitio­ners. Large animal ambulatory veterinarians are not likely to use controlled substances with the same frequency as their counterparts working in clinics or companion animal medi­cine. Most large animal practitioners are responsible for track­ing controlled substance use themselves and are less able to rely on support staff for assistance. Transport of controlled substances in ambulatory vehicles provides an additional chal­lenge. Despite these obstacles, it is critical that large animal ambulatory practitioners place the appropriate amount of im­portance and urgency on managing controlled substances. Un­derstanding federal and state laws, setting up sound handling protocols, and diligent contemporaneous recording are all criti­cal components to developing an easy to manage, DEA-compli­ant policy for managing controlled substances.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 120-122
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
